paragraphs.body: A Fork in the Road
Chapter One

Julie awoke to her breath in her throat. Her alarm had startled her, and her breath was caught, caged and fluttering above her chest. She wanted to sit up, but she couldn’t. Whether it was the alarm or her hitched breath, she didn’t know, but it was like something was perched - no, was crushing - her chest, her lungs moving up and down against the struggle.

annotations.comment: Small point, but I’d only describe her breath once.
annotations.context_right: in her throat. Her alarm had startled her, and her breath was caught, caged and fluttering above he

annotations.comment: In general, you’ve slightly over described this feeling, moving from a feeling that her breath is caught to that she’s being crushed. Can be tightened up for clarity and impact.
annotations.context_left: n her throat. Her alarm had startled her, and her breath was caught, caged and fluttering above her
annotations.context_right: . She wanted to sit up, but she couldn’t. Whether it was the alarm or her hitched breath, she didn’t

paragraphs.body: She reached for her phone, and after three or four rounds of her flailing her arm around, she finally managed to hit the “stop” button.

annotations.comment: “And after three or four flailing attempts, she managed to finally…”

paragraphs.body: The music stopped, and the world was quiet. Little brown birds flitted past her window, slowing their pace as they neared the glass before kicking up speed and catapulting themselves into the cool, September air. A slight breeze greeted Julie at the window pane. It circled the nearest tree and billowed the leaves, still green and sunkissed from the summer heat. The sky was already a brilliant blue, clear and bright with a few clouds dotting the horizon. The world was still; it was almost perfect.

annotations.comment: I was very confused by the beautiful day described in this paragraph, followed by the noisy, unpleasant world described in the next.

paragraphs.body: For Julie, the world was already on fire. Clashes and clatters sounded from every corner of her bedroom, from every corner of the world. A thousand cars were honking from a thousand different directions. Hundreds of people littered the streets, a mass so crowded that you could barely see the pavement. Everything was bustling. Everything was loud. Everything was in motion. And Julie’s heartbeat quickened, pulsing and pulsing as it tried to keep up.

annotations.comment: I just want to note that we’re a page in, and she’s still just laying in bed.
annotations.context_left: thing was bustling. Everything was loud. Everything was in motion. And Julie’s heartbeat quickened,
annotations.context_right: and pulsing as it tried to keep up.

paragraphs.body: She still couldn’t quite sit up; her chest was too heavy. The weight of it all kept her there, fastening her down. So Julie just laid there, with her back pressing into the mattress, her palms scrunched up and refusing to unclench. She tried to think, but the noise, the clashes, the clatters, clouded any and all lines of thinking, and she just sat there and tried to breathe and let all of the noise wash over her.

annotations.comment: She really can sense this much  chaos outside from her room but needed the alarm to wake her up?
annotations.context_left: She still couldn’t quite sit up;
annotations.context_right: chest was too heavy. The weight of it all kept her there, fastening her down. So Julie just laid th

paragraphs.body: Julie closed her eyes and imagined someone pouring a bucket of water over her. The water crashed over her like a clap of thunder. It trickled down her shoulders and raced down the front of her shirt, etching lines into her bare skin. It was like a cool, comforting blanket. She welcomed it as the last drops of water ran through her hair before falling into a patch of grass below. And the noise stopped. And the cars and the people disappeared. And the world was truly quiet.

annotations.comment: Is this a technique she uses to fight anxiety?
I’m having trouble following if the noise outside is real or not?

paragraphs.body: Without hesitation, Julie pulled herself out of bed, her shirt peeling away from her bedsheets. She reviewed the to-list in her head, and before she could even stop it, that struggle, that same invisible struggle, pulled itself up onto her bed and clawed its way to her. Her chest grew taut as the weight of the struggle crushed her once again.

annotations.comment: You’re really describing anxiety (I think!) well, but this feels too short to be the first chapter. The plot hasn’t really gotten started here — we’ve seen that she’s ver anxious but not why it’s triggering for her today.

paragraphs.body: And with it still clinging to her, Julie closed the door to her bedroom and made a beeline for the kitchen as she tried to begin her day.
Chapter Two

Luca was tall. Not so tall that Julie had to crane her neck up at him to look at him, but tall enough. Just the right amount of tall was how Julie had always put it. He had dirty blonde hair that sometimes draped just above his eyelids. He used to have bleach blonde hair as a kid (Julie and Luca had spent a Sunday morning over spring break one year at his house leaning over one of the family photo albums), and if Julie looked hard enough, she could still see bright, yellow strands buried alongside his darker curls. When he smiled, he smiled with his teeth, which Julie liked. Open-mouthed smiles always felt the most genuine to her.

annotations.comment: This feels so real.
annotations.context_left: her neck up at him to look at him, but tall enough. Just the right amount of tall was how Julie had
annotations.context_right: put it. He had dirty blonde hair that sometimes draped just above his eyelids. He used to have blea

annotations.comment: I would note that if you want them to break up, this is a warm and loving way for her to be thinking of him, which is going to be slightly confusing.

paragraphs.body: He had a very calming, very comforting presence about him. Julie often had trouble describing it to her friends. Whenever they asked, she always said that how she truly felt was difficult to put into words. No matter what she said, she could never quite capture the same feelings she had when she was with him.

annotations.comment: Do her friends not know him?

paragraphs.body: That normally happened with a lot of things, though. Julie would describe a summer vacation or an awkward interaction with a stranger she’d never see again to her friends as they sat clustered around their rickety dining room table. And every story, every amusing anecdote, would never turn out the way she’d imagined it would: carefully woven, each and every detail trickled intricately into the story. No matter what, Julie finished every tale with the sense that she had messed it up somehow, that there were still holes she had to fill.

annotations.comment: This is a powerful idea, and I suspect many people will relate to it. But I wonder if we could see some of this in a scene instead of being told it by the narrator?
annotations.context_left: of things, though. Julie would describe a summer vacation or an awkward interaction with a stranger
annotations.context_right: ’d never see again to her friends as they sat clustered around their rickety dining room table. And

paragraphs.body: In Luca’s case, Julie would normally resort to what she always said about him: how happy she was when she saw him, how loved and safe she felt, how at ease she was when she was with him. How sometimes her heart was pounding so hard and so loudly when she thought of him that she could barely catch her breath. All of which were true, but they weren’t the emotions that Julie had felt so deeply, like an ocean with no end in sight. These emotions had been done to death. She had heard them all, time and time again from her other friends with boyfriends. In a way, it felt like stealing, even if it really was how Julie felt.

annotations.comment: I wondered if her friends are really all that interested?
annotations.context_left: In Luca’s case, Julie would normally resort to what she always said about
annotations.context_right: : how happy she was when she saw him, how loved and safe she felt, how at ease she was when she was

paragraphs.body: Julie started back at her laptop, the glow of the screen creating a harsh, white halo around her. She tried to tell herself that she was being productive, but she had, once again, only plugged “Boston” into her search bar and left it at that. That was the seventh time that week that she’d done it, and it was only Tuesday. The cursor blinked at her, as if waiting for her to hit “return.” It was like she could hear it egging her on at each blink: Do it, click it, do it, click it.

annotations.comment: I assumed that she was with Luca, since the chapter begins by noting, as if he’s there, that he’s tall. So I was surprised that she’s actually on her laptop.
Very vivid little scene. I love that she knows how many times she’s done this.
annotations.context_left: waiting for her to hit “return.” It was like she could hear it egging her on at each blink: Do it,

paragraphs.body: Julie let out a long breath as the words repeated over and over to the beat of the cursor. She hit the return key, punching the surface harder than she meant to, and a colorful combination of different images burst onto the screen. She stared at them for a long time, first at the bright, blue skylines before turning to the actual city below. She gazed at the buildings, her eyes fixed on the tallest ones in the center of the city. She zoomed in on monuments and cobblestone streets. Julie typed “Boston Harbor” into her search bar and stared at that for a while, trying to find that instant hit of amazement and wonder looking at the Atlantic Ocean, at the sun dipping below the horizon like a slab of melted butter. But no matter how many photographs, no matter how many sights and sceneries she examined, that instant hit of wonder and amazement never came. Yes, I have to go there! never crossed her mind like she thought it would, and instead, a city rich in history and opportunity only filled her with dread. Her whole body sunk in her chair from the weight of it all.

annotations.comment: I just noticed that cursors don’t blink anymore!

annotations.text: turning to the actual city below.
annotations.comment: I got confused here because this was so similar to the opening when she was looking/sensing the sky.

I think you could possibly give us a teensy bit more information here — help us understand why she’s thinking about moving there. (I find myself trying to remember what you put in your proposal, which, of course, a reader would not have access to!)

paragraphs.body: Boston had been the plan for a long time. It had been all worked out. Well, nothing was official just yet. Julie didn’t have an apartment or even a job there for that matter. But the plan to go to Boston, to move there after graduation, to pursue graduate school at a prestigious university, to live, learn, and work there with Luca, that plan was already set before it even had to be planned. Julie’s search history was littered with names of graduate school programs, of emblems and application fees. She had application portals saved that she could come back to. And best of all, she already knew a familiar face that would be in Boston: Luca.

annotations.comment: I knew what you meant but the wording is confusing.

paragraphs.body: But the more photographs and the more hours spent researching had made her realize that Boston… was a fantasy she didn’t want. A fantasy that would never morph into a reality. Pursuing grad school in Boston was nothing to spit at, Julie knew that. But she also knew, from the moment she and Luca had both talked about it at his apartment last fall, that Boston was purely his idea. And she knew, deep down, that Luca’s vision of Boston would never grow on her. Luca owned that dream, and Julie was just an extension of it.

annotations.text: Boston was nothing to spit at, Julie
annotations.comment: One factor here might be that it’s actually quite difficult to get into those programs.
We need to better understand why she doesn’t want to go to Boston.

paragraphs.body: But Luca didn’t need to know that. Not yet, anyway.
Chapter Three

Julie hadn’t planned on breaking up with Luca. Come to think of it, she had never really thought about it during their relationship. The pair just clicked. They had fit perfectly together and every friend, every acquaintance, every wandering pair of eyes in a local restaurant had thought so too. The two of them had just always worked, with the exception of their most recent conversation over a breakfast of freshly made oatmeal and hot chocolate (Julie had mentioned that it was an odd combination, but Luca had insisted on welcoming the first day of winter with two packets of chocolate powder stirred over some hot water).

annotations.comment: A couple of things to think about here. First, you’re trying that thing that we all do, which is to have your characters be wonderful people. Well, they can be, but we still need to see their flaws. IF she wants to break up with him, something is wrong. Secondly, a smaller point, if they go so perfectly together, why does she need to try to explain to her friends that they work?
I think you have the right idea, but wrong details here. I like the hint that he’s a bit more whimsical (or something) than she is. But Hot Cocoa and Oatmeal actually do go together. :)

paragraphs.body: “I don’t understand,” Luca said, resting his spoon in the oatmeal bowl, the silver handle clamoring against the edge. “I thought this was what you wanted.”
Julie let go of the crinkled napkin in her hand, unaware that she had been toying with it. “It’s not,” Julie said. She tried to reach for something else to say, but she let her sentence end at that.

annotations.comment: I’m confused about the timing here. I thought she wasn’t going to tell him, but here this feels like she is telling him. But this incident feels before the prior scene? Or maybe I’m conflating her decision to definitely not go and this conversation about maybe not going?

paragraphs.body: “Look,” Julie continued once she had found her voice again, “All I’m saying is that I don’t want to jump right into grad school -”

annotations.comment: She seems almost scared of him.

paragraphs.body: “But that’s all you’ve talked about since sophomore year!” Luca said. “You’ve been itching to apply for grad school since I’ve known you.”

Julie let out a long breath, imagining the breath hanging like mist in the air, curling into itself until it eventually formed a cloud. “I know,” Julie said. “I just don’t think that’s what I want anymore.”

annotations.comment: It might be interesting to play with her not being sure of this or not quite knowing what she was going to say until here.

paragraphs.body: She stopped. “I know that’s what I don’t want anymore.”
Luca ran a hand over his eyebrow, slowly massaging his temple as he spoke. “But we had it all planned out.”

It was true. The pair of them had spent nights curled under a blanket, talking about the future. While other people were preparing for their next exam, Julie and Luca were preparing for the rest of their lives, organizing lists of the classes they would take and the textbooks they would have to purchase. They would hover over their separate laptops, carefully examining which classes would fulfill which credits, ensuring that they would both graduate right on schedule. They would discuss potential internships, where they would intern, how they would pay for housing. Study abroad programs left them scrolling through the various options for days on end, and they would return to list-making as they tried to decide which programs they wanted to apply for. The second week of the semester would already consist of advisor meetings, resume workshops, anything that would make the couple feel like they were on top of their academics.

annotations.comment: This seems so specific, which s interesting. I thought that they might be more fantasizing, but this does sound like real planning. (That’s not wrong, it just makes her decision not to do this different.)

paragraphs.body: And then there was grad school. Julie had always known she had wanted to attend. Her parents had encouraged it, so naturally, she gravitated towards the idea of more studying, of extending her academic career into more years abundant with exams, new textbooks, and class discussions that Julie had prepared for. But that fantasy had slipped away the more time she devoted herself to her studies. Textbooks were turning into heavy luxuries, assignments were turning into nuisances, and late night study sessions semester after semester, year after year, were turning into nights spent worrying about the days to come, of all of the things that had to be accomplished, and Julie would wake up in the morning gasping for breath, her chest sinking into herself, as if someone had placed a gigantic animal on top of her. That animal refused to leave her chest as she went through the motions. Its claws would tighten its grip as she brushed her teeth over the sink, digging into her skin as she slipped her pilled sweater over herself. Long walks to the library used to clear her head, as the rain from the night before glistened against the tulips, the golden glow of the sunlight dancing amongst the raindrops. Now, the animal pounded its fists against her chest, quickening her heartbeat. And Julie would spend the day pushing through her work, trying to pull the animal off of her sweater, shooing it into a corner or onto a nearby bookshelf, but to no avail. The animal remained at her side, growing in its power with each assignment, each paper, each exam, each search for Julie’s sense of self.

annotations.comment: Pin down what kind of grad school. It will help you explain both by why she wanted to attend and why she doesn’t now.
It seemed like she was triggered by the outside noise so I wonder if you can make it more clear that she’s stressed and burnt out from school? (Which is honestly a very good reason not to go)
It’s interesting and worth noting that she completely keeps this hidden from Luca.
annotations.context_left: er chest as she went through the motions. Its claws would tighten its grip as she brushed her teeth
annotations.context_right: the sink, digging into her skin as she slipped her pilled sweater over herself. Long walks to the l

paragraphs.body: “No, you had it all planned out” Julie said, tempted to return to her crinkled napkin. “I want different things now. My whole life has been dedicated to school; it’s been everything to me. My life is more than just school.”

annotations.comment: Noting that she’s not really being fair here (which I like as a character trait!)

paragraphs.body: “So that’s it?” Luca said as he folded his hands into his lap. “You’re never going to go to grad school?”
“I never said I wouldn’t go,” Julie snapped. The air grew silent. “I think I just need a break from academics for a little while. I’ll take a year or two off.”
Luca stirred his oatmeal around, turning the blueberries over with his spoon. “Okay,” he said, sucking in a breath. “And what are you going to do with that year or two off?”
“Ideally, I’d like to travel.” Julie squirmed at how formal she sounded. “I want to go to Europe for a little while, maybe visit my family on the West Coast, and I’ll work.”

annotations.comment: Does she know?

paragraphs.body: “Do you still think you’ll be able to go to grad school after all that?” Luca asked, placing greater emphasis on the last few words.
“Well, I still want to get my Master’s. You know that,” Julie said, though she wasn’t sure if Luca knew that at all anymore.

annotations.comment: Again, give us more information on what kind of Master’s.

paragraphs.body: Luca put his spoon down and ran his hands through his hair, letting it flop in front of his face for a few seconds before pushing it back and looking up at Julie.
“It’s just that our whole plan revolved around grad school. About going to Boston, studying, getting jobs there. We worked so hard for that. I thought we were going to do this together,” Luca said.

annotations.comment: He seems pretty calm for a 22 year old. But I appreciate how hurtful “settling” might be.

paragraphs.body: “I’m just not ready for that part of my life. I don’t want to settle yet,” Julie said.
“Who said anything about settling?” Luca said, tears collecting in his throat. Julie held back, trying to restrain her emotions just a little bit longer.

The conversation continued like that for a while: the back and forth, the questions, the discussion of plans that had begun to break apart like a fork in the road, straying further and further away from a single path. The hot chocolate eventually turned cold, and the oatmeal stuck to the bowl, the peanut butter and fruit lingering in the middle.

Eventually, though, the conversation ended. Tears grew dry and caked on saddened cheeks, dishes were cleared from the kitchen table, and awkward goodbyes were exchanged as Julie left Burlington for the winter break, without Luca.

annotations.comment: You summarized the wrong part of the conversation — we need to see this leave-taking.

paragraphs.body: Julie made her way down the stairs, her suitcase barreling behind her.
Luca was already in the hallway by the door.

annotations.comment: Oh, okay, here it comes.

paragraphs.body: “You sure you don’t need help getting to the airport?” he asked. His voice was quieter, more timid. It was as if he was afraid to ask.
Julie reached the last step of the staircase, the wheels of the suitcase echoing against the tiled floor. “Nope, I’m okay,” Julie said far too quickly. She bit her lip as soon as she said it.
Luca’s eyes, still red and puffy from earlier, welled up again. “It’s not a problem, really.”

annotations.comment: It’s sweet that Luca is a crier. I wonder if Julie is used to that? Did they ever fight before?

paragraphs.body: “I’m fine,” Julie said. “I called an Uber, everything’s fine.” Why was she saying all the wrong things?

“No, really, I can take you,” Luca reached his arm out for her suitcase but then pulled back. He tucked a loose strand of hair behind his ear as if to cover his gesture. But Julie had already noticed.
Julie huffed out a sigh. She wanted to punch herself for doing that. “Luca, it’s fine. I can take care of myself.” She lugged the suitcase closer to the door.
Luca scoffed. “I know that,” he said. “But it’s just,” he paused. “We were gonna go together anyway.” Another pause. “Like…before-”
“The Uber’s almost here,” Julie said, cutting him off. She brushed right past him and opened the door. “It’d be stupid to cancel it now.”
“Jules, please,” Luca said, even quieter than before.
“It’s fine, it’s fine,” Julie replied. “I need to go. I should go.” The words tumbled out of her mouth as if they were the easiest words in the world. She bit her lip again.

annotations.comment: Again, it’s good that she’s really being awful to him.

paragraphs.body: “Jules, come on,” Luca said gently. “Please, just let me drive you.”

Julie stood on the porch of the apartment. She stared at Luca’s figure in the threshold. His tall, confident demeanor was gone. In its place was someone new, someone vulnerable. She wanted to take a step toward him, comfort him, tell him that this all wasn’t real and that she’d see him in the New Year, but she didn’t. Her feet remained fixed in their spot, her boots practically fastened to the gravel.

annotations.comment: I’m having a little trouble following if she intended to break up with him or just not go to Boson with him? Could be either.

paragraphs.body: Julie closed her eyes, and for a moment, she wasn’t shivering or trying to hold back tears on her own porch. For a moment, Luca was standing posed in the doorway, surprising her with a homemade strawberry shortcake (her favorite) for her 22nd birthday. For a moment, Julie and Luca were walking home from Julie’s cousin’s wedding, Julie donned in a tea length baby blue dress and Luca in a handsome tux, something she had almost never seen him in. For a moment, it was their first official date, and Luca was walking Julie up her apartment steps, his hand inching closer to Julie’s hand.

annotations.comment: I totally buy that she has conflicted feelings. But right now, it’s hard to see why she’s breaking up with him at all.

paragraphs.body: Julie pulled the strap of the suitcase up out of its compartment. She checked her Uber. It was two minutes away, just rounding the corner onto her street. Julie sniffled and hit the cancel button. What was $30 anyway?
“Okay,” was all she could muster.

annotations.comment: I love how her action here makes it clear that she isn’t sure.

paragraphs.body: A slight smile crossed his lips. A closed smile.
He crossed over the threshold and onto the porch. He reached for her suitcase, more quickly and more sure of himself this time.
“Um… I’ve got it,” Julie jumped in and brought her suitcase closer to her.
“Are you-”
“It’s fine,” Julie said. She tugged her suitcase behind her, her boots stamping clear footprints into the slushy snow.

Luca’s gray Honda Accord was parked on the street. Julie and Luca walked to it, keeping an awkward distance between each other. Luca unlocked the car, and Julie practically sprinted to the trunk, pulling it open as quickly as she could.
As Julie angled her suitcase into the trunk, Luca appeared from the driver’s side of the car.
“But I can do-” Luca started.
“No,” Julie said. She didn’t mean for her tone to be firm, but it was. “It’s okay.”

annotations.comment: Unless you have more information to share, this scene goes on a little longer than needed.

paragraphs.body: Luca drew back, his hands up in surrender. “Alright,” he said. He made his way back to the driver’s seat.

As the car rumbled down the street, Julie tried not to look at Luca, but no matter how hard she tried to look out the window, she couldn’t help but steal glances at him when he wasn’t looking. She secretly hoped he was doing the same.

annotations.comment: I enjoyed this excerpt, Luna. I’m interested in Luca and Julie and kind of want them to work it out or at least better understand why they can’t, which is a good place for a reader to be 10 pages in. My notes really are about my above instinct that you’re separating your character’s thoughts from her actions a bit much, and I’d also like to see you show why Julie doesn’t want to move to Boston — right now, there’s a sort of hole in the center of the plot because we know she won’t go but we don’t really understand why (which makes Luca seem like the sensible, relatable one). So I would give her a reason why — it might not be the real reason or even one that makes sense, but something that she’s saying is the reason. But more importantly, keep writing! You’re off! It’s begun!
